one_line_takeaway: A structured framework for arguing (and adversarially contesting) that an advanced AI system is safe to deploy, organizing evidence into four argument types - inability, control, trustworthiness, deference - and showing that none of today's sixteen building-block arguments is simultaneously practical, strong, and scalable.
key_finding: The four argument types form a capability-tolerance progression: as systems grow more powerful, developers will lean first on inability, then control, then trustworthiness, and eventually deference (trusting credible AI advisors). Crucially, NONE of the sixteen building-block arguments earns full marks on all three axes (practicality, maximum strength, scalability); the arguments the authors rate highest overall are monitoring, externalized reasoning, and testbeds. Dangerous-capability evaluations (the basis of inability arguments) rate strong on practicality and maximum strength but weak on scalability. Strong, highly scalable safety cases - especially trustworthiness arguments like 'not alignment faking' and 'eliciting latent knowledge' - will require fundamental research advances not yet achieved. The paper also argues that safety cases alone are dangerously incomplete and should be paired with adversarial 'risk cases' produced by third-party red teams (putting AI 'on trial'), plus continuous monitoring with revocable certifications.
core_contribution: The paper introduces a framework for constructing and evaluating an AI 'safety case' - a structured, evidence-backed rationale that a deployed AI 'macrosystem' is unlikely to cause a catastrophe (probability below an acceptable threshold, e.g. 0.1%). Adapting the safety-case practice used in six UK-regulated industries and traditional analyses like FMEA, the authors (1) propose a six-step procedure for structuring a safety case (define the macrosystem and deployment decision; specify unacceptable outcomes; justify deployment assumptions; decompose the macrosystem into subsystems; assess unilateral subsystem risk; assess interacting-subsystem/macrosystem risk), summarized in Goal Structuring Notation (GSN) flowcharts; (2) taxonomize safety arguments into FOUR categories - inability, control, trustworthiness, and deference - ordered by how much system capability they must tolerate; (3) enumerate sixteen concrete 'building-block' argument examples and rate each on practicality, maximum strength, and scalability; and (4) give institutional recommendations for regulators/companies using safety cases. This is the paper's own synthesis, not a survey of prior work, though it explicitly builds on and contrasts with prior governance proposals (Anderljung et al., Koessler & Schuett, Schuett et al.).
threat_model: Catastrophic risk from advanced/frontier AI systems during deployment (including internal deployment), where 'catastrophe' is large-scale devastation of specified severity (e.g. billions in damages or thousands of deaths). Concrete threat vectors named include: misuse (terrorists eliciting a bioweapon 'cookbook' from AI assistants), autonomous AI acquiring destructive power (profitably surviving/replicating on the internet, self-exfiltration), AI exfiltrating weights of other misaligned systems via server compromise, and 'alignment faking' where a capable model deliberately behaves well during evaluation while waiting for an opportunity to misbehave. Adversaries are both malicious human users (jailbreaking, adding scaffolding) and potentially the AI systems themselves (sandbagging capabilities, gradient hacking to resist fine-tuning elicitation).
limitations: By the authors' own framing this is a scaffolding/starting point, not a ready-to-use standard: many argument templates depend on unsolved research (reliable capability elicitation, interpretability, latent-knowledge extraction), and the practicality of the whole approach hinges on red teams being 'sufficiently competitive' with real adversaries - an assumption the paper admits is hard to justify given unbounded scaffolding progress and adversary creativity. The framework is qualitative: probability aggregation over GSN nodes and thresholds like 0.1% are illustrative rather than empirically calibrated, and no dataset, tool, or worked numerical safety case is validated against reality. Deference arguments rest on future AI advisors that current systems fall 'far short' of being. The paper offers a vocabulary and structure but does not demonstrate that any real frontier system passes a complete safety case.

quantitative_results: Acceptable catastrophe-probability threshold given as an illustrative target of 0.1% over a deployment window
Four safety-argument categories: inability, control, trustworthiness, deference
Six-step framework for structuring a safety case (modeled on FMEA)
Sixteen example building-block arguments, each rated on three axes (practicality, maximum strength, scalability)
Dangerous-capability evaluations rated: practicality strong, maximum strength strong, scalability weak
Highest-ranked building blocks across all three axes: monitoring, externalized reasoning, testbeds (none achieves full marks)
Illustrative proxy-task threshold: AI achieving >50% accuracy on a bioweapon-development question dataset flags a dangerous capability
'Moderate' scalability defined as automating >=50% of economically-weighted cognitive tasks in a top-ten-GDP country
Safety-case practice drawn from six UK-regulated industries
